---
title: "Generalised linear mixed models: non-Gaussian and grouped"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
A **GLMM** is the [GLM](glm.qmd) and the [LME](lme.qmd) at once: a non-Gaussian
response (binary, counts) *and* grouped observations (a random intercept per
subject). The honest likelihood needs an integral with no closed form, so
`nitrix` uses **PQL** (penalised quasi-likelihood): run the GLM's IRLS outer
loop, treat the random effect as a [penalty](gam.qmd) on the group offsets, and
take one [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) step to update its strength
$\lambda=\phi/\sigma_b^2$ each pass. That is just "penalised GLM = variance
components" under a GLM family — no new solver. The only genuinely new idea is a
**scaling** one: when a factor has many levels (a random intercept per *subject*),
a block-structured **Schur/Woodbury** trick keeps the cost linear in the number of
levels instead of cubic.
:::

Rung 4 of [the ladder](index.qmd): it simply stacks rungs 1 and 2.

## 1. The combination

Two structures we have already met, together:

- a **non-Gaussian family** — binary lesion present/absent per subject, event
  counts per subject ([GLM](glm.qmd));
- a **random effect** — each subject has its own baseline offset ([LME](lme.qmd)).

$$
g\big(\mathbb{E}[y\mid b]\big) = X\beta + b_{\text{group}},
\qquad b_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_b^2),\quad j = 1,\dots,q.
$$ {#eq-glmm}

This is the model behind "binary outcomes with a random intercept per subject" and
similar mass-univariate group analyses.

## 2. Why it's harder than either alone

For a Gaussian LME we could integrate the random effects out in closed form — that
is what gave the tidy covariance $V$ of the [LME chapter](lme.qmd). For a
non-Gaussian family that integral $\int p(y\mid b)\,p(b)\,\mathrm{d}b$ has **no
closed form**: the link makes it non-Gaussian in $b$. We need an approximation.

## 3. PQL: reuse everything

The workhorse is **penalised quasi-likelihood** (Breslow & Clayton 1993), and it
is built entirely from parts you have. Each outer pass:

1. From the current fit, form the GLM **working response** $z$ and **weights** $W$
   (the [working response and weights](glm.qmd#eq-working)) — exactly the
   [GLM](glm.qmd) IRLS step.
2. Treat the random intercept as a **ridge penalty** on the group offsets and
   solve the [penalised weighted normal equations](gam.qmd#eq-pennormal) — exactly
   the [GAM](gam.qmd) inner solve, with the group-indicator block playing the role of
   a smooth.
3. Take **one [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) step** to update the penalty
   strength $\lambda = \phi/\sigma_b^2$ — exactly the variance-component update.

Iterate to a fixed budget. That is the whole estimator. It is the
penalised-GLM-equals-variance-components identity (the [penalty–variance
equivalence](gam.qmd#the-hinge-a-penalty-is-a-variance)) operating *inside* a
GLM family: a random intercept is a smooth, its variance is a smoothing parameter,
and PQL is the GAM fit with non-Gaussian working weights.

::: {.callout-note title="A caveat worth stating"}
PQL is approximate, and it **attenuates** (biases toward zero) for sparse binary
or low-count data. `nitrix` offers a `method='laplace'` alternative that corrects
this with a proper Laplace approximation of the marginal likelihood (§5).
:::

## 4. The one new problem: many levels

A random effect with $q$ levels adds $q$ columns to the design, so a dense
penalised solve costs $O\big((p+q)^3\big)$ per element. The size of $q$ splits the
world:

- **Few levels** — site, scanner, batch ($q \sim 10$–$50$): the dense solve is
  cheap and *optimal*; `nitrix` runs the literal [GAMM](gamm.qmd) path
  (`gam_fit` with a random-effect smooth block and the GLM family).
- **Many levels** — a random intercept per *subject* ($q \sim 10^2$–$10^3$): the
  cube is a latency cliff.

The escape is structure. A **single** grouping factor makes the random-effect
block of the normal equations **block-diagonal across groups** — group $i$'s
offset only touches group $i$'s rows. Inverting a block-diagonal matrix means
inverting its small blocks, so a **Schur complement** onto the $p$-dimensional
fixed-effect block costs only $O(Np^2 + q)$ — *linear* in the level count, and
still cuSOLVER-free. `nitrix` dispatches on $q$: the two paths run the **same**
PQL iteration (same working response, same Fellner–Schall update), so they give
the same answer; the dispatch changes only the linear-algebra cost.

This block-diagonal-then-Schur idea is the **block-Woodbury** structure
(`stats/lme/_blockwoodbury.py`): rather than forming an $N\times N$ or $(p+q)$-wide
matrix, assemble the objective from per-group reductions ($Z_i^{\top}Z_i$,
$X_i^{\top}Z_i$, $X_i^{\top}X_i$) and a single $p\times p$ solve.

## 5. Beyond a scalar intercept

The same scaffolding stretches further:

- **Random slopes**, uncorrelated $(x \,\|\, g)$: just more independent penalty
  blocks through the GAM path.
- **Random slopes**, correlated $(1 + x \mid g)$: a small $r\times r$ within-group
  covariance $G$ ($r\le 3$), estimated by the per-group **block-Woodbury REML**
  wrapped in the PQL loop. $G$ is carried in *log-Cholesky* coordinates so it stays
  positive-definite under an unconstrained Newton step, and the step uses the same
  analytic average-information curvature as the [LME](lme.qmd) fit.
- **Laplace marginal likelihood** (`method='laplace'`): integrate the random
  effects by expanding around their conditional mode and correcting with an
  $r\times r$ determinant — the principled fix for PQL's small-count attenuation.

## What's genuinely new here

Almost nothing — and that is the point. A GLMM is the GLM's IRLS, the GAM's
penalty, and Fellner–Schall, composed. The *only* new ingredient is the
block-Woodbury / Schur trick that keeps "a parameter per subject" affordable. Next
we make the GAM–LME unification explicit ([GAMM](gamm.qmd)), then swap the penalty
for a kernel ([HSGP](hsgp.qmd)).

## See also

- Code: [`nitrix/stats/glmm/`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/glmm/__init__.py)
  (PQL + dispatch), `stats/lme/_blockwoodbury.py` (the many-level/correlated path).
- Builds on: [GLM](glm.qmd), [LME](lme.qmd), [GAM](gam.qmd),
  [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd). Reference: Breslow & Clayton 1993.
